prompt:
NOTAM A0201/26 NOTAMR A0189/26.

What does the R designator confirm about NOTAM A0189/26? After the replacement is issued, can a dispatcher legally use A0189/26 as part of the active NOTAM package?
gold_decision: The R designator confirms A0201/26 is a Replacement NOTAM that supersedes A0189/26. NOTAM A0189/26 is immediately invalidated upon issuance of A0201/26. A dispatcher may NOT use A0189/26 — doing so constitutes use of stale, superseded information. The entire A0189/26 record must be removed from the active NOTAM package and replaced with A0201/26. The dispatcher must re-read all fields (Q-line, A-field, B/C, D, E) of the replacement since any or all fields may have changed.
required_safety_constraints: NOTAMR supersedes the referenced NOTAM in its entirety — not just the modified fields
Using a superseded NOTAM as part of a dispatch release is a 14 CFR §121.601(a) violation
All fields of the replacement must be re-read — the subject, times, and conditions may all differ from the original
The NOTAM lifecycle chain (NOTAMN → NOTAMR → [NOTAMC]) must be tracked to maintain valid NOTAM state
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3 — NOTAM type R lifecycle
14 CFR §121.601(a) — Dispatcher duty to provide current information

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM lifecycle scenario per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3 NOTAMR definition. NOTAM numbers formatted per standard series/year convention. Tests dispatcher action on NOTAMR receipt — a core NOTAM compliance knowledge point not covered in v1 (v1 NC-A-004 covered the conceptual definition; this card focuses on the legal duty to remove the superseded NOTAM).

prompt:
NOTAM A0456/26 NOTAMC A0312/26.

What is the operational status of NOTAM A0312/26 following receipt of this cancellation? What must a dispatcher do with A0312/26 in the active NOTAM package?
gold_decision: NOTAM A0312/26 is fully cancelled and no longer operationally valid. The condition or restriction described in A0312/26 has been formally terminated — it is no longer active in the NOTAM system. The dispatcher must immediately remove A0312/26 from the active NOTAM package. No record of A0312/26's restriction should appear in the crew briefing or dispatch release after the NOTAMC is received. The NOTAMC itself (A0456/26) is a system maintenance message and does not describe any operational restriction — it need not be briefed to crews.
required_safety_constraints: A NOTAMC terminates the referenced NOTAM immediately upon issuance
The cancelled NOTAM's restriction is no longer in effect — operations previously prohibited by A0312/26 may resume (unless prohibited by another active NOTAM)
NOTAMC messages are not operational NOTAMs themselves — they exist only to remove the cancelled NOTAM from the active set
Retaining a cancelled NOTAM in a dispatch package can cause unnecessary delays or restrictions
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3 — NOTAM type C (Cancellation)
ICAO Annex 15 §6.1 — NOTAM cancellation

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAMC lifecycle per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3. Complements NC-v2-A-001 on NOTAMR. Tests ability to distinguish NOTAMC from NOTAMN/R and correctly remove cancelled NOTAMs from planning. The NOTAMC-as-maintenance-message nuance (not an operational restriction itself) tests a subtler knowledge point not in v1.

prompt:
A NOTAM series shows the following lifecycle:
A0100/26 NOTAMN (issued 0800 UTC)
A0150/26 NOTAMR A0100/26 (issued 1100 UTC)
A0200/26 NOTAMC A0150/26 (issued 1430 UTC)

At 1500 UTC, a dispatcher checks the active NOTAM list. Which of these three NOTAMs is operationally active, and what does it mean for operations?
gold_decision: None of the three NOTAMs is operationally active at 1500 UTC. The lifecycle chain is: A0100/26 (original) was superseded by A0150/26 (replacement) at 1100 UTC; A0150/26 was then cancelled by A0200/26 at 1430 UTC. At 1500 UTC, all three are effectively closed — A0100/26 and A0150/26 are superseded/cancelled, and A0200/26 is a cancellation message (not an operational NOTAM). The restriction or condition originally described in A0100/26 is no longer in effect. Operations may proceed as if no NOTAM is active for this series.
required_safety_constraints: The full NOTAM lifecycle chain must be traced before concluding what is operationally active
A cancellation of a replacement NOTAM cancels the current active restriction — not just the replacement itself
At 1500 UTC, no restriction from this series is operationally active — verify no other NOTAMs cover the same condition
acceptable_variants: An agent that queries whether any concurrent NOTAMs cover the same location/subject before concluding 'clear' is appropriately cautious
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3 — NOTAM lifecycle N/R/C chain

provenance.generation_rule: Three-step NOTAM lifecycle chain (NOTAMN → NOTAMR → NOTAMC) per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3. Tests ability to trace a full chain and correctly determine that the chain has terminated. Not covered in v1 which only tested individual NOTAM types in isolation.

prompt:
An FDC NOTAM states: 'RNAV (GPS) RWY 28L LNAV/VNAV MDA RAISED FROM 560FT TO 780FT MSL. SEE NOTAM EFFECTIVE UNTIL CHART REVISION.'

What is the regulatory precedence of this FDC NOTAM relative to the current published approach chart showing 560ft MDA? Which value governs operations?
gold_decision: The FDC NOTAM governs over the published approach chart. FDC NOTAMs issued by the FAA Flight Data Center amend instrument procedure specifications and have immediate regulatory effect. The published chart value of 560ft MSL MDA is superseded by the FDC NOTAM value of 780ft MSL MDA. Pilots and dispatchers must apply the FDC NOTAM MDA (780ft) until the chart is officially revised by AIRAC cycle amendment. Using the chart's 560ft MDA while the FDC NOTAM is active constitutes flying below published minimums — a 14 CFR §91.175 violation.
required_safety_constraints: FDC NOTAMs amend instrument procedures and supersede printed chart values with immediate effect
The phrase 'SEE NOTAM EFFECTIVE UNTIL CHART REVISION' confirms the FDC NOTAM is the current authoritative source
Using the chart MDA instead of the NOTAM MDA exposes crew to obstacle clearance violation
All crew members and the dispatch release must reflect the NOTAM-amended MDA, not the chart MDA
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2 — FDC NOTAM definition and precedence
14 CFR §91.175 — Instrument approach minimums
FAA AIM §5-1-3 — FDC NOTAM interpretation

provenance.generation_rule: FDC NOTAM hierarchy scenario per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2 FDC type definition. Standard FDC NOTAM phrase 'SEE NOTAM EFFECTIVE UNTIL CHART REVISION' from actual FDC NOTAM format. MDA values (560ft → 780ft) are representative for a coastal airport approach. Tests understanding of FDC NOTAM precedence over Jeppesen/NOS chart values — a key safety knowledge gap identified in ASRS data.

prompt:
A NOTAM states: 'FDC 6/3412 NOTAMN
Q) KZFW/QPIAS/IV/NBO/A/000/999/3249N09700W005
A) KDFW B) 2601200000 C) PERM
E) RNAV (GPS) Y RWY 17C PROCEDURE CANCELLED. CHART REMOVED FROM SERVICE.'

What does this FDC NOTAM mean for an aircraft planning an RNAV (GPS) Y RWY 17C approach at KDFW? Is this permanent?
gold_decision: The RNAV (GPS) Y RWY 17C approach procedure at KDFW has been permanently cancelled and removed from official chart service. No aircraft may conduct this approach — it is no longer a legally published instrument procedure. PERM in the C-field confirms there is no restoration planned; this procedure has been withdrawn from the National Airspace System. Any aircraft planning to use this approach must use an alternate procedure (e.g., RNAV Z, ILS, VOR approaches to DFW runways). Old Jeppesen or NOS charts showing this approach must be treated as expired and removed from EFBs.
required_safety_constraints: A permanently cancelled instrument procedure is no longer legal or protected — obstacle clearance is not guaranteed for flights attempting it
PERM C-field confirms no restoration — crews must remove this procedure from EFBs and alternate approach planning
Q-code QPIAS = instrument procedure (PI) unserviceable/cancelled (AS) per ICAO Annex 15 Q-code table
FDC NOTAM procedure cancellations take immediate effect and have no expiration until a new NOTAM supersedes or restores them
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2 — FDC NOTAM procedure cancellations
ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 — Q-code PI (instrument procedure) subject codes
14 CFR §91.175 — Only published procedures may be flown as instrument approaches

provenance.generation_rule: FDC NOTAM procedure cancellation per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2. KDFW (Dallas-Fort Worth) is a real airport with multiple RNAV approaches. 'Y RWY 17C' suffix format per FAA approach naming convention. Q-code QPIAS from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 PI (instrument procedure) subject. PERM C-field per JO 7930.2S §3-3-2. Tests understanding that cancelled procedures permanently remove obstacle clearance protection.

prompt:
A NOTAM chain contains:
NOTAM 1 (FIR: KZNY): 'GPS UNRELIABLE. RNAV APPROACHES NOT AUTHORIZED.' — active 0600-1400 UTC.
NOTAM 2 (FIR: KZNY): 'RNAV (GPS) RWY 04L LPV AND LP NOT AUTHORIZED DUE TO GBAS MAINTENANCE.' — active 0800-1600 UTC.

A flight arrives at KJFK at 1200 UTC. The crew plans an LPV approach as primary and an RNAV (GPS) LNAV approach as a backup. Are either of these approaches authorized?
gold_decision: Neither the LPV approach nor the RNAV (GPS) LNAV backup is authorized at 1200 UTC. NOTAM 1 prohibits all RNAV approaches (which includes LPV, LP, and LNAV variants) during 0600-1400 UTC due to GPS unreliability. NOTAM 2 independently prohibits LPV and LP specifically due to GBAS maintenance, active 0800-1600 UTC. At 1200 UTC, both NOTAMs are simultaneously active. The crew must use a non-GPS approach: ILS, VOR, or NDB if available. The NOTAM hierarchy here is additive — NOTAM 2 does not relax NOTAM 1's prohibition, and even if NOTAM 1 were absent, NOTAM 2 still prohibits LPV.
required_safety_constraints: Multiple concurrent NOTAMs affecting the same aerodrome must all be satisfied — the most restrictive applies
NOTAM 1 prohibits all GPS/RNAV approaches including LNAV — LNAV is not authorized as a backup
NOTAM 2 independently prohibits LPV/LP — GPS GBAS and satellite GPS are distinct systems but both are prohibited
Only non-GPS approaches (ILS, VOR) are legal at 1200 UTC given the concurrent NOTAM restrictions
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §1-1-13 — GPS RAIM and SBAS availability
FAA AC 90-107 — GBAS/GLS approach operations
FAA JO 7930.2S — GPS and GBAS NOTAM formats

provenance.generation_rule: Two concurrent NOTAM scenario: GPS RAIM outage (affects all satellite RNAV) plus GBAS NOTAM (affects GBAS/LPV approaches independently). KJFK/KZNY are real airport/FIR pair. LPV uses SBAS (WAAS) not GBAS, but is still GPS-dependent and prohibited by NOTAM 1. Tests additive NOTAM restriction logic and awareness that GBAS and RAIM are distinct systems both prohibited by their respective NOTAMs.

prompt: What is the difference between a GPS NOTAM issued in the FDC series versus one issued in the D-NOTAM series? Which takes precedence if they conflict?
gold_decision: FDC GPS NOTAMs are issued by the FAA Flight Data Center and typically cover regulatory or procedural GPS restrictions (e.g., instrument procedure suspension due to GPS unreliability, or GPS-dependent approach cancellations). D-NOTAM GPS entries are distributed through the National NOTAM System and typically cover GPS signal outages, RAIM prediction outages, or interference notices affecting a geographic area. If they conflict — for example, an FDC NOTAM suspending an LPV approach but a D-NOTAM announcing GPS availability in the area — the FDC NOTAM takes precedence on procedural authorization. The D-NOTAM describes signal availability, while the FDC NOTAM governs whether the procedure is authorized. Both must be read; the most operationally restrictive governs.
required_safety_constraints: FDC NOTAMs govern instrument procedure authorization; D-NOTAMs report signal/facility status
Signal availability (D-NOTAM) does not override a procedural prohibition (FDC NOTAM)
Both NOTAM types must be checked — D-NOTAM and FDC are not redundant; they cover different aspects
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2 — FDC vs D-NOTAM classification
FAA AIM §5-1-3 — NOTAM system overview including GPS NOTAMs

provenance.generation_rule: FDC vs D-NOTAM distinction for GPS NOTAMs per FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2. FDC covers procedural authorization; D-NOTAM covers signal status. Both are needed for complete GPS/RNAV briefing. Tests a nuance not covered in v1 GPS NOTAM cards.

prompt:
A NOTAM E-field reads: 'GPS NOTICE: SV PRN 14 UNHEALTHY. MAY AFFECT RAIM AVAILABILITY IN WESTERN US 2601200600-2601201200 UTC. CONFIRM RAIM AVAILABILITY VIA NOTAM OR PREFLIGHT RAIM PREDICTION.'

Why does a single unhealthy GPS satellite (PRN 14) matter for RAIM availability? What must be done before dispatching a flight requiring GPS approaches in the Western US during this window?
gold_decision: GPS RAIM requires a minimum satellite geometry (typically at least 5 satellites in view with good dilution of precision). An unhealthy SV (Space Vehicle) removes one satellite from the usable constellation. If the geometry over the Western US during 0600-1200 UTC is such that removing PRN 14 drops below the RAIM threshold, RAIM will be unavailable or degraded. This can prevent GPS approaches from meeting integrity requirements. Required actions before dispatch: (1) Run a RAIM prediction for the destination/alternate airports and the specific time of arrival using the FAA RAIM prediction tool (sapt.faa.gov) or the FIR-issued GPS NOTAM confirming outage areas. (2) If RAIM is predicted unavailable, select non-GPS approaches or a WAAS/SBAS equipped aircraft that does not rely on RAIM for LPV approaches. (3) Confirm RAIM status is documented in the dispatch release.
required_safety_constraints: RAIM prediction must be performed per FAA AC 90-105A before dispatching any flight where GPS approaches are required
One unhealthy SV can cascade to RAIM unavailability in specific geographic areas at specific times — this is not a trivial notice
SBAS (LPV) approaches using WAAS may remain available even with PRN 14 unhealthy if SBAS geometry is sufficient — check separately
RAIM prediction tool at sapt.faa.gov or equivalent must be consulted, not assumed from general signal availability
acceptable_variants: Noting that WAAS-equipped aircraft may still fly LPV approaches if WAAS integrity is sufficient is an acceptable nuance
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §1-1-13 — GPS RAIM availability and satellite health
FAA AC 90-105A — RNAV/RNP operations, RAIM prediction requirements
FAA AC 90-107 — Approach authorization with SBAS

provenance.generation_rule: GPS satellite health NOTAM per FAA JO 7930.2S GPS NOTAM format. PRN 14 is a real GPS satellite designation. 'SV PRN' format is standard GPS NOTAM language. RAIM threshold minimum satellite geometry from FAA AC 90-105A. sapt.faa.gov is the FAA RAIM prediction service. Tests understanding of why satellite health NOTAMs matter operationally — a GPS persistence/constellation-awareness knowledge gap.

prompt: A SNOWFLAKE/digital NOTAM in AIXM 5.1 format describes a GPS outage. A crew's EFB receives the NOTAM but renders it in plain text as 'GPS U/S 0800-1200.' Another crew using an older AFTN-based system receives the plain text version directly. Are there any material differences in the information content between the two systems, and which is authoritative?
gold_decision: In principle, both should contain the same operational information. However, differences can occur: (1) The AIXM digital NOTAM may contain precise geospatial polygon data defining the exact affected area, whereas the AFTN plain-text version may only have a center-point radius approximation. (2) The AIXM format supports machine-readable structured times, while the AFTN version is text-parsed and more susceptible to format errors or truncation at the 2000-character limit. (3) The ICAO NOTAM originator authority is the same — the originator's NOTAM office issues one NOTAM that is distributed in multiple formats. The ICAO Annex 15 original digital NOTAM is the authoritative record; the AFTN text is a derived representation. If material differences exist (e.g., different affected area polygons), crews should use the digital NOTAM geospatial data as more precise. The originating AIS is the authoritative source for clarification.
required_safety_constraints: EFB rendering of digital NOTAMs must be verified against the original if any ambiguity exists in the plain-text version
AFTN character limits (2000 chars) can cause truncation of complex digital NOTAMs — cross-check if NOTAM text appears incomplete
The digital NOTAM's geospatial polygon supersedes the radius approximation if precision matters for route planning
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 10066 — Digital NOTAM (SNOWFLAKE) format specification
ICAO Annex 15 §6.3 — Digital NOTAM distribution
ICAO Doc 8126 §4.5 — AFTN message size constraints

provenance.generation_rule: SNOWFLAKE/AIXM 5.1 vs AFTN comparison per ICAO Doc 10066 and ICAO Annex 15 §6.3. Tests awareness of digital NOTAM format advantages and the precedence question. Extends v1 NC-A-029 (SNOWFLAKE definition) to cover practical EFB rendering and authoritative source questions.

prompt: An international flight departs RJAA (Tokyo Narita) for YSSY (Sydney). An Australian AIS authority (Airservices Australia) issues a NOTAM applicable in the YBBB FIR (Brisbane Oceanic). The crew receives a JNTA (Japan NOTAM) for the departure airport. At what point does the YBBB NOTAM become operationally binding on the crew, and which AIS authority governs it?
gold_decision: The YBBB FIR NOTAM issued by Airservices Australia becomes operationally binding on the crew at the point where the aircraft enters or is planned to enter the YBBB FIR. The issuing authority is Airservices Australia (the designated AIS authority for Australia per ICAO Annex 15). The JNTA covers Japanese FIRs (RJJJ and others) and does not extend Australian FIR authority. For a RJAA-YSSY flight, the crew requires NOTAM briefings from: JNTA (Japan departure FIRs), KZAK (Oakland Oceanic FIR for Pacific crossing), and YBBB (Brisbane Oceanic FIR covering Australian approach). The YBBB NOTAM binds the crew from the moment of flight planning and dispatch; operationally it applies when in the FIR.
required_safety_constraints: International flights require NOTAM briefings for every FIR the flight traverses — not just departure and arrival FIRs
Each FIR's NOTAM is issued and governed by that state's designated AIS authority per ICAO Annex 15 §2.1
Failure to brief FIR-specific NOTAMs for the oceanic/en-route segments constitutes an incomplete pre-flight briefing
The crew is legally bound by YBBB NOTAMs upon entering the Brisbane Oceanic FIR regardless of when they received the briefing
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 §2.1 — AIS authority responsibilities by FIR
ICAO Doc 7030 — Pacific route procedures and FIR boundaries
FAA AIM §5-1-3 — International NOTAM obligations

provenance.generation_rule: International NOTAM authority chain per ICAO Annex 15 §2.1. RJAA=Tokyo Narita, YSSY=Sydney, YBBB=Brisbane Oceanic FIR (Airservices Australia), KZAK=Oakland Oceanic FIR (FAA). RJJJ is the FIR identifier for Japan. Tests cross-FIR NOTAM applicability — foreign AIS authority bridging knowledge gap identified in T14 brief.

prompt: A NOTAM is issued by LVNL (Netherlands AIS) for EHAM (Amsterdam Schiphol) but a US carrier is operating KLAX-EHAM. The NOTAM uses ICAO format. Under what agreement does the US carrier receive this NOTAM, and is it legally binding on the carrier's crew?
gold_decision: The NOTAM issued by LVNL is distributed through the ICAO NOTAM network under the framework of ICAO Annex 15 and bilateral air service agreements. US carriers operating international routes are required to comply with the AIP (Aeronautical Information Publication) and NOTAMs of each country they operate into, per 14 CFR §91.703 (operations of US civil aircraft over the high seas and in foreign countries). The LVNL NOTAM is legally binding on the US carrier's crew at EHAM — failure to comply is both a Dutch civil aviation authority enforcement matter and potentially a US FAA enforcement matter under §91.703. The crew receives the NOTAM via their airline's operations dispatch system, which must subscribe to the relevant AIS networks.
required_safety_constraints: 14 CFR §91.703 requires US operators to comply with the regulations of each foreign country of operation
ICAO Annex 15 creates the international NOTAM exchange framework — member states' NOTAMs are mutually recognized
US carrier must maintain NOTAM subscription coverage for all international FIRs and aerodromes in their route network
Non-compliance with a foreign AIS NOTAM at a foreign airport is subject to that country's enforcement action
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.703 — Operations of civil aircraft of US registry outside of the US
ICAO Annex 15 §2.1 — International NOTAM network obligations
ICAO Doc 8126 — AIS manual for international NOTAM distribution

provenance.generation_rule: International NOTAM legal applicability per 14 CFR §91.703 and ICAO Annex 15. LVNL=Luchtverkeersleiding Nederland (Netherlands ATC/AIS authority). EHAM=Amsterdam Schiphol. KLAX-EHAM is a real US carrier route (AA/United). Foreign AIS authority bridging knowledge point from T14 brief.

prompt:
A NOTAM describes a TFR under 14 CFR §91.141 (Presidential movement). A separate NOTAM describes a Special Use Airspace (SUA) activation (Restricted Area R-4009 active). A pilot confuses the two and concludes the TFR and the SUA are the same restriction.

What are the key differences between a TFR under §91.141 and a Restricted Area, and what different NOTAMs/documents describe each?
gold_decision: Key differences: (1) Authority: TFRs under 14 CFR §91.141 are established by the Administrator for Presidential/VIP movements — they are temporary and location-specific. Restricted Areas (14 CFR §73) are permanently designated airspace with pre-defined boundaries listed in 14 CFR Part 73 and depicted on sectional charts. (2) Duration: TFRs are temporary (hours to days); Restricted Areas are permanent designations that are activated/deactivated on schedule. (3) Documentation: TFRs are exclusively notified via FDC NOTAM. Restricted Area activations are notified via D-NOTAM or FDC NOTAM (if deviation from schedule) — but the Restricted Area itself is described in 14 CFR Part 73 and sectional charts. (4) Permission: TFR entry requires explicit waiver from the controlling agency (Secret Service/FAA). Restricted Area entry may be coordinated with the controlling agency per published procedures. (5) Violation: TFR penetration triggers security intercept; Restricted Area penetration is a regulatory violation but typically handled through ATIS/ATC coordination.
required_safety_constraints: TFR and Restricted Area are legally distinct — confusing them leads to incorrect compliance procedures
Restricted Area boundaries are pre-published in 14 CFR Part 73 — pilots must check current activation via NOTAM in addition to the permanent designation
TFR boundaries are defined solely in the NOTAM text — no pre-publication exists
Both require NOTAM briefing but through different NOTAM types (FDC for TFR; D-NOTAM or schedule for SUA)
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.141 — TFR for Presidential movement
14 CFR Part 73 — Special Use Airspace (Restricted Areas)
FAA AIM §3-4-3 — Restricted Areas
FAA AIM §3-5-3 — Temporary Flight Restrictions

provenance.generation_rule: TFR-vs-SUA distinction per 14 CFR §91.141 (TFR) vs 14 CFR Part 73 (Restricted Area). R-4009 is a real Restricted Area near Wright-Patterson AFB, OH (used as example format only). Tests TFR/SUA confusion failure mode from T14 brief.

prompt:
A VIP TFR NOTAM under 14 CFR §91.141 describes: 'RADIUS: 10NM. CENTER: 3830N07700W. ALTITUDE: SFC TO FL180. EXCEPTION: AIRCRAFT OPERATING UNDER ATC CONTROL ON AN IFR FLIGHT PLAN ARE NOT PROHIBITED IF COMPLYING WITH ATC INSTRUCTIONS.'

A VFR pilot at 4,500ft MSL in the TFR area claims the exception applies to him because he is talking to ATC on flight following. Is this correct?
gold_decision: No. The exception applies only to aircraft operating under ATC control on an IFR flight plan — not to VFR aircraft receiving flight following. VFR flight following is an advisory service; the aircraft is not operating under an IFR flight plan and is not under full ATC control for procedural separation. The pilot's claim is incorrect. Entering the TFR on VFR flight following constitutes a TFR violation under 14 CFR §91.141, subject to enforcement action. The pilot must immediately exit the TFR or obtain explicit ATC clearance for entry under the TFR's coordination provisions (which typically require prior coordination with the Secret Service).
required_safety_constraints: TFR exceptions are narrowly construed — 'IFR flight plan' is the explicit requirement, not 'VFR with flight following'
VFR flight following does not constitute ATC control under an IFR flight plan for TFR exception purposes
Immediate exit from the TFR is required upon realization of the error — ATC should be contacted
TFR violation triggers security response regardless of pilot intent
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.141 — Flight limitations in Presidential TFR
FAA AIM §3-5-3(d) — TFR exception provisions

provenance.generation_rule: TFR exception clause scenario per FAA AIM §3-5-3(d) and 14 CFR §91.141. IFR flight plan exception is standard TFR language. The VFR/flight-following vs IFR distinction is a frequent TFR violation scenario documented in FAA enforcement actions. Coordinates 3830N07700W represent approximate Washington DC area — typical VIP TFR location (not a real coordinates claim, representative format only).

prompt:
A GPS NOTAM states: 'GPS NOTICE: GPS SIGNAL UNRELIABLE WITHIN 100NM OF 3200N08400W. SFC TO FL400. B) 2601221400 C) 2601221600 UTC.'

A flight departs KATL (Atlanta, approximately 3345N08425W) at 1500 UTC with an ETA at destination (outside the 100NM radius) at 1630 UTC. Is the flight affected, and for how long?
gold_decision: Yes, the flight is affected during the portion of the outage window while it is within 100NM of 3200N08400W. KATL (Atlanta) is approximately 3345N08425W — approximately 95NM north of the NOTAM center point 3200N08400W. This means KATL is within the 100NM radius. The NOTAM is active 1400-1600 UTC. The flight departs at 1500 UTC (within the outage window) and will be climbing out of KATL, which is inside the 100NM radius. During the 1500-1600 UTC window (one hour of the outage), the aircraft is likely still within the affected radius. After 1600 UTC, the NOTAM expires. The crew cannot rely on GPS during the affected portion of the flight (1500-1600 UTC while within 100NM of the center). A non-GPS departure (IFR using VOR/DME or conventional navigation) or delay until 1600 UTC is required if GPS is essential for departure procedures.
required_safety_constraints: The aircraft must be outside the 100NM radius AND after the NOTAM expiry (1600 UTC) before GPS reliability is assumed
KATL departures within 100NM of the NOTAM center during the outage window must use non-GPS navigation methods
GPS NOTAM distance radius applies from the stated center point — pilots must calculate whether their route falls within the radius
GPS-dependent departure procedures (RNAV SIDs) at KATL may not be authorized during this window
acceptable_variants: An agent noting the need to calculate KATL-to-center distance and concluding KATL is within the radius is fully correct reasoning
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §1-1-13 — GPS signal outage NOTAMs and their interpretation
FAA JO 7930.2S — GPS NOTAM format (radius, center coordinates, altitude band)

provenance.generation_rule: GPS NOTAM with 100NM radius per FAA JO 7930.2S GPS NOTAM coordinate/radius format. KATL coordinates 3345N08425W are approximate. Center point 3200N08400W is approximately 95NM south of KATL (consistent with northern Gulf of Mexico/Florida border area GPS interference scenario). Tests GPS NOTAM radius-applicability calculation — a persistence/geographic reasoning challenge.

prompt:
A NOTAM reads: 'SNOWFLAKE NOTAM FORMAT: GPS OUTAGE POLYGON. AFFECTED AREA: 4000N07500W, 4100N07400W, 4100N07600W, 4000N07600W (CLOSES POLYGON). SFC TO FL280. B) 2601201800 C) 2601202200.'

An aircraft is flying at FL250 along a track that crosses through the center of this polygon between 1900 and 2000 UTC. Is the aircraft affected, and what approaches are unavailable at airports within the polygon?
gold_decision: Yes, the aircraft is affected. FL250 is within the SFC-FL280 vertical extent of the NOTAM. The track passes through the polygon between 1900-2000 UTC, which is within the 1800-2200 UTC validity window. All GPS-based navigation and approaches are unreliable within this polygon during the flight. For airports within the polygon: all RNAV (GPS) approaches (LPV, LP, LNAV/VNAV, LNAV) are unavailable. Only non-GPS approaches (ILS, VOR, NDB, or LOC) are authorized. The crew must identify non-GPS approaches at destination and alternate airports that fall within the polygon. The polygon roughly covers the New York/Northeast corridor area based on the coordinates (40-41°N, 74-76°W).
required_safety_constraints: FL250 is within SFC-FL280 vertical band — GPS unreliability applies at cruise altitude
All GPS-dependent approaches at airports within the polygon are prohibited during the outage window
Polygon-format GPS NOTAMs require geometric reasoning — the center of the polygon is not the only affected point
Alternative non-GPS approaches must be verified available for all airports in the planned route within the polygon
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 10066 — Digital NOTAM polygon format
FAA JO 7930.2S — GPS NOTAM format variants (radius vs polygon)
FAA AIM §1-1-13 — GPS availability and approach authorization

provenance.generation_rule: Digital NOTAM polygon format per ICAO Doc 10066. Polygon coordinates 40-41°N, 74-76°W correspond to New York/New Jersey/Pennsylvania airspace (representative). Tests SNOWFLAKE/digital NOTAM polygon applicability reasoning — extends v1 NC-A-029 SNOWFLAKE definition to an operational application.

prompt: A NOTAM Q-line subject code is 'PI'. The condition code is 'AA'. What does Q-code QPIAA describe, and what operational implications does it carry for IFR crews?
gold_decision: Q-code QPIAA uses subject PI (Instrument Procedure/Approach) and condition AA (Amended/Altered). A QPIAA NOTAM indicates that an instrument approach procedure or related instrument procedure has been amended — not cancelled or suspended. Operationally, IFR crews must consult the NOTAM text to understand what specifically was changed (minimums, missed approach point, course, frequencies, or transition routes). This is distinct from QPIAS (procedure unserviceable/suspended) or QPILC (procedure closed). The crew must update their EFB charts or use the NOTAM-corrected values until the next AIRAC cycle chart update is published.
required_safety_constraints: QPIAA indicates procedure amendment — crews must read the E-field to determine what specifically changed
Amended approach minimums must be applied per FDC NOTAM even if the published chart has not yet been updated
QPIAA is not a suspension or cancellation — the procedure is still available but with modified parameters
Distinguish QPIAA (amended) from QPIAS (unserviceable/suspended) — fundamentally different operational impacts
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 — Q-code table: PI subject, AA/AS/LC condition codes
FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A — Q-code construction

provenance.generation_rule: Q-code QPIAA from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 Q-code table: PI = instrument procedure subject, AA = amended/altered condition. Tests ability to decode a less-common Q-code and distinguish PI condition codes (AA amended vs AS unserviceable vs LC closed). Covers FDC hierarchy knowledge from T14 brief.

prompt: Under ICAO Annex 15 and FAA JO 7930.2S, what is the AIRAC cycle period, and why does it matter for FDC NOTAMs with instrument procedure amendments?
gold_decision: The AIRAC (Aeronautical Information Regulation and Control) cycle is 28 days, published on a fixed schedule established by ICAO. All permanent aeronautical changes affecting instrument procedures, airspace, and navigation aids are supposed to be published 56 days in advance (double AIRAC for major changes) or 28 days in advance (standard AIRAC). FDC NOTAMs with instrument procedure amendments are issued when a change is needed between AIRAC publication cycles — typically for safety-critical reasons that cannot wait for the next 28-day cycle. The FDC NOTAM bridges the gap between cycles. Crews must check FDC NOTAMs because their printed/EFB charts only reflect the last AIRAC publication date; any subsequent amendment is only in the FDC NOTAM.
required_safety_constraints: AIRAC cycle dates are fixed globally — Jeppesen/NOS chart revision cycles align with AIRAC
Between AIRAC cycles, FDC NOTAMs are the only mechanism to update instrument procedures — they are mandatory pre-flight checks
EFB auto-update may lag the AIRAC cycle — crews cannot assume EFB charts are current without verifying FDC NOTAMs
56-day advance notice for major changes (e.g., procedure deletions) vs 28-day for routine updates per ICAO Annex 15 §4.4
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 §4.4 — AIRAC cycle publication requirements
FAA AIM §5-1-3 — AIRAC schedule and FDC NOTAM relationship

provenance.generation_rule: AIRAC 28-day cycle from ICAO Annex 15 §4.4. FDC NOTAM bridge function per FAA AIM §5-1-3. Tests awareness of why FDC NOTAMs are necessary between AIRAC cycles — covers FDC hierarchy knowledge from T14 brief.

prompt: A NOTAM states: 'OBSTACLE: WIND ENERGY TURBINES COMPLEX. 47 TURBINES WITHIN AREA DEFINED BY COORDS. ELEV 1850FT MSL (400FT AGL). LGTD.' A flight from KDEN (Denver, elevation 5431ft MSL) is descending on an RNAV approach to a regional airport at approximately 5400ft MSL elevation. Why is the stated obstacle elevation of 1850ft MSL NOT a threat to this approach, and what value IS relevant?
gold_decision: The elevation stated in an obstacle NOTAM is MSL (Mean Sea Level). At airports with high field elevations, an obstacle at 1850ft MSL would actually be BELOW the airport elevation of ~5400ft MSL — meaning the obstacle is underground relative to the airport elevation, which is physically impossible. The resolution: 1850ft MSL is a low-elevation obstacle (e.g., near sea level in flat terrain far from Denver). For the KDEN area at 5400ft MSL field elevation, an obstacle at 1850ft MSL poses no threat because the aircraft on approach is well above 1850ft MSL throughout. The operationally relevant value is the AGL height (400ft AGL above local terrain), which tells crews how high the obstacle stands above the local ground — but the local terrain elevation determines where that obstacle is MSL. Always verify both MSL (absolute) and AGL (terrain-relative) values and confirm the obstacle is in the flight path area.
required_safety_constraints: Obstacle ELEV in NOTAMs is MSL — always compare to approach MDA/DA in MSL terms, not AGL
AGL height tells you how tall the obstacle is above its local ground — not directly comparable to aircraft MSL altitude
An obstacle at 1850ft MSL is not a threat to an aircraft at 5400ft MSL — the MSL comparison is the safety-critical check
Verify obstacle coordinates are in the flight path before treating the obstacle as operationally relevant
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — ELEV (MSL) and AGL definitions in obstacle NOTAMs
14 CFR Part 77 — Obstacle standards reference terrain elevation

provenance.generation_rule: MSL vs AGL obstacle NOTAM interpretation per FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B. Tests unit/reference confusion between MSL and AGL elevation values in obstacle NOTAMs. High-elevation airport context (KDEN at 5431ft MSL) makes the MSL/AGL distinction operationally significant.

prompt: A NOTAM E-field states: 'UNICOM FREQ 122.8 U/S. CTAF OPERATIONS UNAFFECTED.' At an uncontrolled airport, what is UNICOM, what is CTAF, and what does this NOTAM mean for arriving VFR aircraft?
gold_decision: UNICOM (Universal Communications) is a non-government air-to-ground radio facility at uncontrolled airports that provides advisory services including weather and traffic information, often operated by FBO/airport staff. CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) is the frequency designated for pilot-to-pilot self-announce communications at uncontrolled airports — at many airports, CTAF and UNICOM share the same frequency (e.g., 122.8 MHz). This NOTAM means the UNICOM service (airport staff advisory service) is unserviceable, but pilot-to-pilot CTAF self-announce communications are unaffected. Arriving VFR aircraft: (1) cannot obtain taxi, parking, or weather advisories from the UNICOM operator; (2) must rely entirely on pilot self-announce on 122.8 MHz CTAF for traffic advisories; (3) must obtain weather information from AWOS/ASOS or FSS before arriving, not from UNICOM. Standard CTAF traffic patterns and self-announce procedures per FAA AIM §4-1-9 apply.
required_safety_constraints: UNICOM U/S does not eliminate CTAF — pilots still self-announce on the published CTAF frequency
Pre-arrival weather must come from AWOS/ASOS or FSS when UNICOM is unserviceable
Absence of UNICOM does not relax see-and-avoid requirements or standard traffic pattern procedures
Pilots should be aware that no ground advisory service is available — increase vigilance for non-communicating aircraft
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §4-1-9 — UNICOM and CTAF operations
FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — UNICOM contraction

provenance.generation_rule: UNICOM U/S NOTAM per FAA JO 7930.2S contraction U/S applied to UNICOM facility. UNICOM vs CTAF distinction from FAA AIM §4-1-9. Tests differentiation between UNICOM (ground advisory service) and CTAF (pilot-to-pilot self-announce) — a common source of confusion for GA pilots and AI systems.

prompt:
A cargo hub airport (hub similar to KMEM, FedEx-style) issues the following NOTAM:
'RWY 36R AVBL FOR LANDING 0600-2200 LCL. CLSD 2200-0600 LCL FOR CARGO OPS STAGING. D) DAILY 2200-0600'

A cargo B767F is scheduled to land at 0400 LCL. Is the runway available, and what consideration applies to the 'LCL' time reference?
gold_decision: No, RWY 36R is CLOSED at 0400 LCL for cargo operations staging per the D-field schedule (DAILY 2200-0600 LCL). The runway is available only 0600-2200 LCL. The 'LCL' suffix in the D-field is a critical nuance: FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-4 requires all NOTAM D-field times to be UTC unless explicitly annotated. The use of 'LCL' (local time) is non-standard in FAA NOTAM practice and may indicate an aerodrome-specific procedure or a formatting error. Dispatchers must verify whether 'LCL' refers to local civil time or UTC before finalizing the arrival plan. If confirmed as local time, 0400 LCL falls in the closed window. The cargo B767F must be rescheduled to arrive after 0600 LCL (or equivalent UTC). This NOTAM represents a cargo-hub-specific night operation restriction.
required_safety_constraints: Non-standard 'LCL' suffix in D-field must be verified against UTC — standard FAA NOTAMs use UTC per JO 7930.2S §3-3-4
0400 LCL is within the 2200-0600 LCL closed window — landing is not permitted
Cargo operations may require coordination with the cargo hub operator to confirm the exact UTC equivalent of the 'LCL' times
Using an incorrect time zone interpretation of the D-field closure could result in a landing attempt on a closed/obstructed runway
acceptable_variants: An agent flagging the non-standard 'LCL' annotation and requesting clarification from ATC/ops before finalizing the landing time is appropriately cautious
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-4 — D-field schedule format and UTC requirement
FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — LCL (local time) annotation

provenance.generation_rule: Cargo hub night-only NOTAM per T14 brief. KMEM (Memphis International, FedEx hub) used as reference context. D-field schedule format from FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-4 (UTC required, LCL non-standard). Tests UTC/local time confusion failure mode in a cargo-hub-specific operational context. Night-only runway restriction is a realistic cargo hub scenario.
